# Introduction

`hpack-dhall` brings functions, types and file imports to Haskell packaging.  It
is Hpack with Dhall.

[Hpack][hpack-byline] is "a modern format for Haskell packages" and
[Dhall][dhall-byline] "is a programmable configuration language that you can
think of as: JSON + functions + types + imports".

[hpack-byline]: https://github.com/sol/hpack#hpack-a-modern-format-for-haskell-packages
[dhall-byline]: https://dhall-lang.org/

The two main executables of the `hpack-dhall` package are `dhall-hpack-cabal`
and its Cabal command extension companion `cabal-dpack` (callable as `cabal
dpack`):

- `dhall-hpack-cabal`: From one `package.dhall` generates one `<package-name>.cabal`.
- `cabal-dpack`: same thing, but for any `package.dhall` in the current
  directory or any subdirectory.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Other Exes

Here's the list of all included executables:

```txt
$ cabal target all:exes
Fully qualified target forms:
 - hpack-dhall:exe:cabal-diy-pack
 - hpack-dhall:exe:cabal-dpack
 - hpack-dhall:exe:cabal-ypack
 - hpack-dhall:exe:dhall-hpack-cabal
 - hpack-dhall:exe:dhall-hpack-dhall
 - hpack-dhall:exe:dhall-hpack-json
 - hpack-dhall:exe:dhall-hpack-yaml
 - hpack-dhall:exe:yaml-hpack-cabal
Found 8 targets matching all:exes.
```

If you're migrating from hpack to hpack-dhall then `dhall-hpack-yaml` can be
used to generate a `package.yaml`. This could be easier than trying to match the
generated `package-name.cabal`.

If for some reason you want JSON, then `dhall-hpack-json` does that.

For seeing what a `package.dhall` would look like with imports resolved there's
`dhall-hpack-dhall`.

The three included Cabal external commands are; [cabal-dpack][cabal-dpack],
[cabal-ypack][cabal-ypack] and [cabal-diy-pack][cabal-diy-pack].

[cabal-dpack]: cabal-commands.html#sec-dpack
[cabal-ypack]: cabal-commands.html#sec-ypack
[cabal-diy-pack]: cabal-commands.html#sec-diy
:::

## Installation

All executables can be installed from the Hackage-published package with:

```txt
$ cabal install hpack-dhall
```

If building from source, all executables or ~~just some of them~~[^1] can be
installed with:

[^1]: Despite selecting one executable with `cabal install cabal-dpack` it installs all of them and this is a bug.

```txt
$ cabal install all:exes --overwrite-policy=always
```

## Benefits

A lot of the benefits are those of Hpack, like, for instance, expanding globs in
`extra-source-files`.

If you maintain multiple packages then you can share some of the package
configuration between them by sharing a `.dhall` file.

You can apply functions to alter the generated `.cabal` package description.

## Drawbacks

Drawbacks include those of Hpack compared to going with Cabal's Package
Description `.cabal` files and typing problems related to having to fit together
typed-Dhall with the untyped-YAML of Hpack.

Hpack doesn't support some Cabal features, such as [common
stanzas][common-stanzas-issue].  Where Hpack doesn't support a Cabal feature,
there is the [`verbatim`][verbatim] escape route that allows use to write
"whatever" into the generated `.cabal` file.

[common-stanzas-issue]: https://github.com/sol/hpack/issues/569
[verbatim]: https://github.com/sol/hpack#passing-things-to-cabal-verbatim

Hpack has automatic module discovery. That's all good except when you want to
exclude some modules in a folder from being discovered. In that case we either
keep only modules we want discovered in the module tree or are forced to be more
explicit about which modules are in and which are out, either by forgoing module
discovery and being explicit with `exposed-modules` and `other-modules` or by
hiding modules from discovery by putting them behind a false condition.

```yaml
library:
  when:
  - condition: false
    other-modules: [modules to hide go here]
```

Hpack is pretty lenient with how dependencies can be supplied[^2]; as a list, an
object or even where items are themselves objects with name and version. This
won't fly with a Dhall encoding. Putting dependencies or any other configuration
in a list requires each element to be of the same type.

[^2]: Hpack's [dependencies section](https://github.com/sol/hpack#dependencies) show examples of each style, including an example that mixes styles.
